title: Weather hazards — tropical storms and extreme UK weather
examTip: AQA Geography Paper 1, Topic 1 (The Challenge of Natural Hazards). Expect questions on tropical storm formation, structure, primary/secondary effects, and a case study comparison (LIC vs HIC). UK extreme weather is often tested in shorter 4-mark questions. Typhoon Haiyan 2013 is your go-to LIC case study.
introTips: Covers global atmospheric circulation and why tropical storms form where they do
You'll be able to explain the structure and effects of tropical storms with precision
By the end you can compare LIC and HIC responses to tropical storms — a common 6–8 mark task
intro: Every year, tropical storms cause billions of pounds of damage and kill thousands of people. Knowing why they form, where they strike, and why some countries cope far better than others is exactly what AQA wants you to explain. This topic also connects directly to climate change — understanding atmospheric circulation helps you explain why extreme weather is changing.

keyPoints.heading: Global atmospheric circulation: why the wind blows where it does
keyPoints.content: The atmosphere circulates in a pattern of three cells per hemisphere, driven by differences in solar heating. The Hadley Cell sits between 0° and 30°. At the equator, intense solar heating causes air to rise, creating the Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) — a belt of low pressure, cloud, and heavy rainfall. This rising air spreads out and descends at around 30° north and south, creating high pressure belts — this is why the world's major hot deserts sit at 30° (e.g. Sahara, Arabian Desert). The Ferrel Cell sits between 30° and 60°, with surface winds blowing towards the poles (these are the Westerlies). The Polar Cell sits above 60°. Trade Winds blow from 30° towards the equator (northeast in the northern hemisphere, southeast in the southern hemisphere) as air returns to fill the rising zone at the ITCZ. This global pattern determines where rainfall falls, where deserts form, and — crucially — where tropical storms develop.
writeBullets: Hadley Cell — 0–30°; hot air rises at equator (ITCZ), descends at 30° creating deserts
Ferrel Cell — 30–60°; surface Westerlies blow poleward
Polar Cell — 60–90°; cold air descends at poles
ITCZ — Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone; belt of rising air, cloud, and heavy rain at equator
Trade Winds — blow from 30° toward equator; drive tropical storm tracks
Deserts at 30° — descending dry air creates high pressure and low rainfall

keyTerms.def: Atmospheric circulation cell from equator to 30°; hot air rises at equator, descends at 30°
Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone; a belt of low pressure and heavy rain along the equator
Surface winds that blow from 30° back toward the equator, driving tropical storm movement
Mid-latitude circulation cell (30–60°) with surface Westerly winds

keyPoints.memTip: Three cells per hemisphere: Hadley (hot equator), Ferrel (middle), Polar (cold poles) — H, F, P from equator to pole. Cover your notes and write each cell's location, the direction of surface winds, and one key effect (ITCZ, deserts at 30°, etc.).
keyPoints.tutor_note: You don't need to recall every detail of circulation for most questions, but you do need to explain WHY deserts are at 30° (descending dry air from Hadley Cell) and WHY tropical storms form between 5–20° (warm ocean near ITCZ where Trade Winds converge). Linking the two ideas scores highly.

keyPoints.heading: Tropical storms: formation conditions and structure
keyPoints.content: Tropical storms are known as hurricanes (Atlantic/Eastern Pacific), typhoons (Western Pacific), or cyclones (Indian Ocean/Australasia) — they are the same phenomenon. They form when four conditions are met: sea surface temperature above 27°C (to provide the heat energy and water vapour that fuels the storm); the location is between 5° and 20° from the equator (closer to the equator lacks enough Coriolis effect; further away, sea surface temperatures drop); low wind shear (winds at different altitudes must be similar speed, otherwise the storm breaks apart); and the Coriolis effect (Earth's rotation deflects the rising air, causing the storm to spin). The structure has three key parts: the eye (calm centre, low pressure, clear skies, ~30–50 km wide), the eye wall (surrounds the eye; strongest winds, most intense rainfall, highest risk), and spiral rain bands (bands of cloud and rain spiralling outward from the eye wall). Storms weaken when they move over land (cut off from warm water energy source) or cooler seas.
writeBullets: Sea temp >27°C — warm water evaporates to fuel the storm with heat and moisture
5–20° from equator — enough Coriolis effect to spin the storm; sea is warm enough
Low wind shear — winds at different heights must be similar to keep the storm organised
Coriolis effect — Earth's rotation causes rising air to spin, forming the storm's rotation
Eye — calm, low pressure centre with clear skies; ~30–50 km wide
Eye wall — strongest winds and heaviest rain; most dangerous zone of the storm

keyTerms.def: A large rotating storm with winds over 120 km/h, forming over warm tropical oceans
The calm, low-pressure centre of a tropical storm with clear skies
The ring of cloud surrounding the eye; contains the most intense winds and rainfall
The deflection of moving air caused by Earth's rotation; causes tropical storms to spin
Difference in wind speed or direction at different altitudes; high shear prevents storm formation

keyPoints.memTip: Formation conditions: 'SLCC' — Sea temp (>27°C), Location (5–20°), Coriolis effect, Calm wind shear. Cover your notes and explain each condition and what would happen if it wasn't met.
keyPoints.tutor_note: A very common exam error is saying tropical storms form 'at the equator'. They don't — at the equator the Coriolis effect is zero and the storm can't spin. The 5–20° range is key. Also, many students describe the eye as the most dangerous part — it's actually calm. The eye wall is the most dangerous.

keyPoints.heading: Effects of tropical storms and how we respond
keyPoints.content: Tropical storms produce devastating primary and secondary effects. Primary effects (immediate results of the storm): strong winds destroy buildings and uproot trees; storm surges — where sea level rises by several metres as low pressure sucks up the ocean — cause coastal flooding and are often the deadliest element; heavy rainfall causes flooding and landslides; infrastructure (roads, power lines) is destroyed. Secondary effects (consequences of the primary damage): disease spreads through contaminated flood water; people are made homeless; economies suffer from lost businesses and tourism; long-term psychological trauma. Responses include four strategies: monitoring (satellites track storm paths; hurricane hunter aircraft fly into storms to measure conditions); prediction (computer models forecast landfall 3–5 days ahead, giving time to prepare); preparation (evacuation plans, building design — e.g. shutters, reinforced roofs, raised buildings); and aid (international and local relief organisations providing food, water, shelter, and medical care after the event).
writeBullets: Storm surge — sea level rises several metres due to low pressure; major cause of death in coastal areas
Primary effects — strong winds, flooding, landslides, infrastructure damage, deaths
Secondary effects — disease, homelessness, economic losses, psychological impacts
Monitoring — satellites and hurricane hunters track storm position and intensity
Prediction — forecast models give 3–5 days warning; allows evacuation orders
Preparation — evacuation plans, reinforced buildings, emergency stockpiles reduce impact

keyTerms.def: A temporary rise in sea level caused by a storm's low pressure and strong winds pushing water onshore
An immediate impact of the storm itself (e.g. wind damage, flooding)
An impact that results from the primary effects (e.g. disease from contaminated water)
Aircraft that flies into tropical storms to collect data on pressure, wind speed and structure

keyPoints.memTip: For responses, remember MPPA: Monitor, Predict, Prepare, Aid. Cover your notes and give one specific example of each response type and explain how it reduces the impact.
keyPoints.tutor_note: In exam questions about responses, always distinguish between short-term (immediate) responses and long-term responses — they may use either phrasing. And remember: the 4 Ps (Monitor, Predict, Prepare, Aid) apply to all natural hazards, not just tropical storms.

keyPoints.heading: Case study: Typhoon Haiyan 2013 (Philippines) vs Hurricane Katrina 2005 (USA)
keyPoints.content: For AQA you need a detailed tropical storm case study. Typhoon Haiyan (2013) struck the Philippines (a Lower Income Country / NEE) in November 2013. It was one of the most powerful tropical storms ever recorded, with wind speeds of 315 km/h. It killed over 6,000 people, displaced 4 million, and destroyed 600,000 homes. The storm surge in Tacloban was over 5 metres. Challenges in responding included: remote islands difficult to reach; weak infrastructure collapsed; international aid was slow to arrive; political issues hindered coordination. Long-term recovery was slow and uneven. By contrast, Hurricane Katrina 2005 struck the Gulf Coast of the USA (HIC), causing around 1,800 deaths and $125 billion in damage. Although warning systems were in place, the New Orleans levee system failed — showing that even HICs can struggle. However, the USA's wealth allowed faster recovery, and the response highlighted inequality (poorer, predominantly Black communities suffered most). The comparison shows that wealth reduces vulnerability but does not eliminate risk entirely.
writeBullets: Typhoon Haiyan 2013 — Philippines (LIC/NEE); 315 km/h winds; 6,000+ deaths; 4 million displaced
Storm surge of 5m+ hit Tacloban city, causing catastrophic coastal flooding
Response challenges — remote islands, collapsed infrastructure, slow international aid
Hurricane Katrina 2005 — USA (HIC); ~1,800 deaths; $125 billion damage; levee failure
HIC advantage — faster recovery, more resources; but inequality visible in who suffered most
Key lesson — wealth reduces vulnerability, but preparation matters more than wealth alone

keyTerms.def: Newly Emerging Economy — a country transitioning from low to middle income (e.g. Philippines)
An embankment built to prevent river or coastal flooding; failed during Katrina in New Orleans
How much harm a population will suffer from a hazard, shaped by wealth, governance, and preparation

keyPoints.memTip: Haiyan: H for Huge destruction (6,000+ dead), Philippines. Katrina: K for Katrina killed 1,800, USA — smaller death toll but massive economic cost. Cover your notes and write five specific facts for each case study.
keyPoints.tutor_note: When comparing LIC and HIC case studies, don't just say 'HICs cope better' — that's too vague. Explain exactly why: early warning systems, building standards, evacuation routes, government capacity. And mention cases where HICs also struggled (Katrina levees) to show nuanced thinking.

keyPoints.heading: UK extreme weather: is it getting worse?
keyPoints.content: The UK is not immune to extreme weather. Events such as the 2013–14 winter floods — when the Somerset Levels were underwater for weeks — and the 2022 heatwave (40.3°C recorded for the first time) suggest weather is becoming more extreme. The causes are linked to jet stream behaviour: the polar jet stream is a fast-moving ribbon of air high in the atmosphere that steers weather systems. When it weakens or becomes more 'wavy' (a looping path rather than a straight one), weather systems stall over one area for longer, producing prolonged rain or prolonged drought. Evidence links jet stream instability to Arctic warming (reducing the temperature difference that drives the jet stream). The social impacts of UK extreme weather include disruption to transport, school closures, and health impacts (hypothermia, heatstroke). Economic impacts include crop damage, flooded businesses, and insurance costs. Environmental impacts include soil erosion, flooding of habitats, and stress on wildlife. Future projections suggest wetter winters, hotter drier summers, and more frequent extreme events in the UK.
writeBullets: 2013–14 winter floods — Somerset Levels flooded for weeks; widespread transport disruption
2022 heatwave — 40.3°C recorded in UK for first time; health and infrastructure stress
Jet stream — high-altitude wind belt that steers UK weather; when wavy, systems stall
Arctic warming — reduces temperature gradient that drives jet stream, causing instability
Social impacts — transport disruption, school closures, health risks
Future projections — wetter winters, hotter drier summers, more frequent extremes

keyTerms.def: A fast-moving ribbon of wind high in the atmosphere that steers weather systems across the UK
Low-lying wetland in southwest England; severely flooded in 2013–14 winter
Weather that is significantly outside the normal range, causing disruption or damage

keyPoints.memTip: Remember SEE for impact categories: Social (people), Economic (money), Environmental (nature). Cover your notes and write two specific impacts in each category for UK extreme weather.
keyPoints.tutor_note: For UK extreme weather questions, always link to climate change — examiners want you to show that you understand this isn't random bad luck, but part of a wider trend. Mentioning jet stream behaviour and Arctic warming will lift your answer above description into explanation.

tables.title: Tropical storm impacts and responses: LIC vs HIC comparison
rows: 600,000+ homes destroyed; roads and ports collapsed
New Orleans levees failed; widespread flooding
Some warning given; many could not evacuate in time due to poverty
International aid slow to arrive; access blocked by damaged roads
FEMA and National Guard deployed; response criticised for delays
Slow and uneven; some areas still recovering years later
Faster due to greater financial resources, though inequalities exposed
Poverty increases vulnerability; remote geography worsened access
Wealth helps, but poor planning and inequality still cost lives

1.question: Why do tropical storms form between 5° and 20° from the equator, rather than at the equator itself?
options: Sea temperatures are higher at 5–20° than at the equator
The equator has no Coriolis effect, so storms cannot spin and form there
There is too much rainfall at the equator to allow storms to develop
Trade Winds are absent at the equator so storms have no energy source
1.correctFeedback: Correct. At the equator, the Coriolis effect is zero — there is no force to start the rotation that a tropical storm needs. Between 5–20°, the Coriolis effect is strong enough to spin the rising air into a storm, while sea temperatures are still above 27°C.
1.wrongFeedback: The key is the Coriolis effect. At the equator, Earth's rotation produces no deflecting force on moving air — so rising air just goes straight up and can't spin into a tropical storm. The 5–20° zone gives enough Coriolis effect to generate rotation while the sea is still warm enough (>27°C) to fuel the storm.

3.question: Typhoon Haiyan (2013) killed over 6,000 people in the Philippines. Which factor MOST explains why the death toll was so high compared to similar storms hitting HICs?
options: Haiyan was much larger than any storm that has hit a HIC
The Philippines had no warning systems at all
Poverty meant people could not evacuate, infrastructure was weak, and emergency response was limited
The storm hit at night when people were asleep
3.correctFeedback: Right. The Philippines had some warning, but poverty meant many could not leave, coastal communities had weak housing, and the government had limited emergency capacity. These socioeconomic factors explain the scale of the disaster far more than the storm's size.
3.wrongFeedback: The Philippines did receive some warning, but poverty is the core explanation. People living in poverty cannot easily evacuate — they may have no transport, nowhere to go, and cannot afford to lose their property. Weak building standards mean homes collapse in storm surges. Limited government capacity slows emergency response. Poverty amplifies the physical hazard into a much greater disaster.

commonMistakes: Saying tropical storms form 'at the equator' — they don't. The Coriolis effect is zero at the equator so storms can't spin; they form between 5° and 20° where Coriolis is present and sea temperatures are still above 27°C.

Describing the eye of a tropical storm as the most dangerous part — the eye is calm with low pressure and clear skies. The eye wall is the most dangerous zone, with the strongest winds and heaviest rain.

Confusing primary and secondary effects — primary effects are the direct results of the storm (wind damage, flooding). Secondary effects are consequences of those primary effects (disease, homelessness). Keep them clearly separated.

Giving vague case study answers — 'the Philippines is a poor country so more people died' earns limited marks without specific data. Use 6,000+ deaths, 315 km/h winds, 5m storm surge, 4 million displaced.

Forgetting that weather and climate are different — weather is short-term (day to day), climate is the long-term average pattern (30+ years). Extreme weather events are evidence for climate change but are not the same as climate.
examTips: For tropical storm formation questions, always give all four conditions (sea temp >27°C, 5–20° location, Coriolis effect, low wind shear) and explain what each one contributes — don't just list them.
Case study answers must include specific named data — death tolls, storm intensity, named places, specific responses. 'A lot of people died' will not earn marks above Level 1.
When evaluating responses to tropical storms, use the four categories: Monitor, Predict, Prepare, Aid — this gives your answer a clear structure and shows you understand the full range of strategies.
For UK extreme weather questions, always link to climate change and the jet stream. Show that you understand this is part of a broader pattern, not just a one-off event.

In a 6-mark comparison question between LIC and HIC responses, aim for at least three clear points of comparison with evidence, and always end with a conclusion about the most important factor explaining the difference.
revisionCardBullets: Three atmospheric cells per hemisphere: Hadley (0–30°), Ferrel (30–60°), Polar (60–90°)
ITCZ — rising air at equator creates low pressure, cloud, and heavy rain
Deserts at 30° — descending dry air from Hadley Cell creates high pressure
Tropical storm formation: sea temp >27°C, 5–20° latitude, Coriolis effect, low wind shear
Storm structure: eye (calm, low pressure), eye wall (strongest winds), spiral rain bands
Primary effects: wind damage, storm surge, flooding, landslides, deaths
Secondary effects: disease, homelessness, economic losses, infrastructure loss
Responses: Monitor (satellites), Predict (computer models), Prepare (evacuation), Aid (relief)
Typhoon Haiyan 2013 (Philippines/LIC): 315 km/h winds, 6,000+ deaths, 4 million displaced
Hurricane Katrina 2005 (USA/HIC): ~1,800 deaths, $125bn damage, levee failure
UK extreme weather: 2013–14 Somerset floods; 2022 heatwave (40.3°C record)
Jet stream instability — Arctic warming reduces temperature gradient, causing wavy jet stream and stalled weather
